coredata.dc:title: Chapter 7 Laser-driven nanomaterials and laser-enabled nanofabrication for industrial applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shujun
Gao, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: As one of the greatest inventions of the 20th century, laser has enabled many revolutionary technologies and still is pushing forward the development of future technologies. Nanomaterials and nanotechnologies are one of the areas in which laser played significant roles. This chapter provides an overview on the laser-assisted synthesis and processing of a variety of nanomaterials as well as laser-enabled nanofabrication. Fundamental mechanisms behind the laser-matter interactions including pulse laser-induced ionization, low-power laser-induced photochemical and photothermal effects are first introduced. Followed by discussion on laser-based methods for the synthesis of different nanoparticles including laser ablation and laser pyrolysis. Laser-enabled nanofabrication including laser projection nanolithography and laser interference nanolithography are also presented. Since carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, carbon dots, and graphene quantum dots have been rising as an important family of nanomaterials attracting tremendous attention and laser is one of the driven forces for their dynamic development. Therefore, a separate section is dedicated to reveal the adoption of laser for synthesis and processing of this particular family of nanomaterials. The industrial application perspectives of each of the laser-driven nanomaterials are also introduced across the chapter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laser
Synthesis of nanomaterials
2D and 3D nanofabrication
Nanomaterials
Carbon-based nanomaterials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Brief on fundamentals behind response of matter to laser
Metal nanoparticles from LA and applications
Metal oxide nanoparticles from LA and applications
Semiconductor nanoparticles from LA and applications
Laser-driven synthesis and processing of carbon-based nanomaterials and applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representations of photoluminescence via spontaneous decay (A) and laser via stimulated emission (B): in the case of photoluminescence, incident photons are absorbed to excite electrons into the excited states followed by natural decay (i.e., electrons return to ground states) emitting a photon of a lower frequency; in the case of stimulated emission, the incident photon come after the excitation (can be realized by different stimuli) which triggers the decay of the electrons with emission of photons. In this case, the incident photons and emitted photons have the same frequency and are also in phase which favors constructive interference increasing the intensity of the resulting light. By applying a pair of a mirror and a semitransmissive mirror to trap and reflect photons, photons can travel in between the mirrors and undergo repetitive stimulated emission processes (i.e., the process as indicated by 1, 2, 3…n in b) until the intensity reach a critical value allowing the light beam (i.e., laser) to pass through the semitransmissive mirror.
Schematic representations of apparatuses applied for gaseous phase (A) and liquid phase (B) laser ablation.
Schematic representation of a typical apparatus for laser pyrolysis.
Schematic representation of the setup for laser projection lithography.
Schematic representation of laser interference nanolithography: patterns are generated by the interference between the reflected part i and part ii of the same laser beam.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 7
chapter.ce:title: Laser-driven nanomaterials and laser-enabled nanofabrication for industrial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Queensland Micro- and Nanotechnology Centre, Nathan Campus, Griffith University, Brisbane, QLD, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Clean Environment and Energy, Gold Coast Campus

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Materials under Shock and Impact, Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Materials under Shock and Impact

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the greatest inventions of the 20th century, laser has enabled many revolutionary technologies and still is pushing forward the development of future technologies. Nanomaterials and nanotechnologies are one of the areas in which laser played significant roles. This chapter provides an overview on the laser-assisted synthesis and processing of a variety of nanomaterials as well as laser-enabled nanofabrication. Fundamental mechanisms behind the laser-matter interactions including pulse laser-induced ionization, low-power laser-induced photochemical and photothermal effects are first introduced. Followed by discussion on laser-based methods for the synthesis of different nanoparticles including laser ablation and laser pyrolysis. Laser-enabled nanofabrication including laser projection nanolithography and laser interference nanolithography are also presented. Since carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, carbon dots, and graphene quantum dots have been rising as an important family of nanomaterials attracting tremendous attention and laser is one of the driven forces for their dynamic development. Therefore, a separate section is dedicated to reveal the adoption of laser for synthesis and processing of this particular family of nanomaterials. The industrial application perspectives of each of the laser-driven nanomaterials are also introduced across the chapter.

ce:section.ce:para: Although laser was invented in 1958 by Charles Hard Townes and Arthur Leonard Schawlow [1], the theoretical foundation of laser was laid by Albert Einstein in one of his famous papers “The Quantum Theory of Radiation” [2]. In a section of this paper, he described the “stimulated emission” which is the theoretical cornerstone of laser. As depicted in Fig. 1 , when a material (a solid, a liquid, or a gas) is excited via any type of stimuli (e.g., light, electric field, etc.), the ground-state electrons are “pumped” into the excited states, those excited electrons will decay into the ground states in a very short time frame (usually in the scale lower than nanoseconds) via two pathways, either spontaneously or triggered by a photon. The former may emit light in the form of fluorescence. Stimulated emission involves the later process which emits photons possessing same frequency as well as in phase with the incident photons which triggered the decay [2].

Since the emitted photons and incident photons are in phase, the intensity (i.e., amplitude) of the light wave is stimulated via constructive interference. Laser is the outcome of the stimulated emission. The intensity of laser could be enhanced to very high levels via repeating this process, which is normally achieved by trapping the stimulated light in between two mirrors. One of the mirrors is semitransmissive (Fig. 1B) which ensures the laser can only escape after being enhanced to targeted level. Therefore, the intensity of the output laser could be tuned by varying the transparency of the semitransmissive mirror.

Laser could be classified into three broad groups depending on the physical states of the active materials—solid state, liquid state, or gas phase, laser from these materials is different in terms of the wavelength, ranges of power, as well as cost. Laser could also be characterized by the modes of operation as two types, continuous or pulse laser. Pulse laser could be realized through techniques such as mode locking, Q-switching, or direct adoption of pulsed pumping sources (e.g., light excitation, electrical discharge, etc.) [3, 4]. Depending on the duration of the pulse, pulsed laser could be further classified into femtosecond, picosecond, nanosecond, etc. lasers. In general, continuous laser has higher coherence (i.e., spatially and temporarily in phase) than pulse laser, but pulse laser is better for heat dissipation during processing. Hence, pulse lasers are mainly used for the synthesis of nanomaterials via technique such as laser ablation (LA) [5–8].

Laser is one of the most important technologies invented in the 20th century. Nowadays, the applications of laser have stretched over a vast spectrum including information technology, optoelectronics, quantum computing, materials characterization, materials processing, advanced three-dimensional (3D) printing, biology, biomedical, etc. In the nanotechnology domain, laser can be used for synthesis, processing, and characterization [3]. In this chapter, we will focus on applying laser for synthesis and processing of nanomaterials as well as nanofabrication.

The main technique using laser for the synthesis of nanomaterials is LA which adopts laser with short pulses for creating nanomaterials from different precursors in gaseous (i.e., vacuum) or liquid phases. The less popular laser pyrolysis for the synthesis of nanomaterials is also discussed. Apart from synthesis, the different end applications enabled by these laser-driven nanomaterials are also introduced.

Thanks to the level of precision has been achieved in control of laser with respect to power, beam, pulse duration, etc., laser is also an ideal tool for processing of nanomaterials including the one-dimensional (1D) or two-dimensional (2D) patterning as well as fabrication of different 3D architectures [9–12]. Besides, synthesis and processing can be achieved at the same time to enable different patterns and structures, therefore, different devices.

Moreover, patterning and establishing of 3D architectures could also be easily realized using laser as a postprocessing tool. For instance, direct laser writing techniques have been applied for fabrication of various graphene patterns [13] and 3D structures have been created with two photon- or multiphoton polymerization techniques [14, 15]. In contrast to the conventional patterning technique such as photolithography, these laser-driven techniques possess advantages including much simpler procedures (almost one-off fabrication in contrast to multiple procedures involved in photolithography), greener processing (no need for etching chemicals and photoresistors, etc.), in situ chemical modification of functional materials during patterning, and so on.

Lastly, as carbon-based materials including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanodiamonds (NDs), graphene and its derivatives [e.g., graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), etc.], and carbon dots (CDs) have become a very important family in the domain of nanomaterials and nanotechnologies with great potential to be used in applications ranging from electronics, composites, energy generation, conversion and storage, catalysis, water purification to biochemical, and biomedical [16–18]. Laser is one of the most important techniques involving in both the synthesis and processing of these carbon-based materials. Therefore, we dedicate an independent section (Section 5) in particular to introduce the use of laser in the synthesis and processing of these carbon-based nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Brief on fundamentals behind response of matter to laser
ce:section.ce:para: Laser-matter interactions underline both the laser-driven synthesis and processing of nanomaterials. They are also of fundamental interest to both experimental and theoretical physicists for understanding laser-induced atomic and molecular dynamics to the extent of quantum mechanical theorization [19]. Many factors have influence on the laser-matter interactions, such as the types (e.g., femtosecond laser, nanosecond-pulsed laser, or continuous laser), intensity (i.e., power density), polarization and shapes of lasers, as well as status of materials (i.e., gas, liquid, nanoparticles, or bulk solids). Most well-known physical and chemical mechanisms behind the laser-matter interactions are the ionization and plasma processes particularly when very short pulsed and high-intensity lasers are used [3]. Recently, terms such as photothermal and photochemical effects are increasingly popular for describing the laser-matter interactions especially for processing of carbon-based materials (e.g., graphene) in which normally low-intensity lasers are applied [13, 20].

High-intensity and short-pulsed laser (e.g., femtosecond laser) can induce extremely strong electric field which is able to push electrons through the Coulomb binding potential thereby ionizing the atoms [3]. The complexity of the ionization process increases when the interactions between the ionized species are taken into account. The dissociated electrons can interact with the ions in the form of “recollision” under the strong electrical field causing multiple electrons ionization [19, 21]. However, due to the oscillation nature of the pulsed laser, the dissociated electrons are confined close to the atoms. Plasma is often an accompany state especially in between pulses, the existence of plasma adds another degree of complexity to the situation, as the charged species also are under influence of the plasma field. Lastly, X-ray can be generated from the recombination of charged species of the plasma [3, 19]. The interactions are of great interest to physicists who are looking to study the dynamic of the atoms and molecules [19].

Recently, low-intensity lasers are increasingly applied for the processing of materials. For instance, different lasers have been adopted for the reduction and patterning of GO to fabricate a variety of devices [13, 20, 22]. The laser-materials interactions are often attributed to two processes photothermal effects and photochemical effects [20, 22]. The former originates from the assumption that laser induces localized high temperatures which can cause deoxygenation of GO [23], the latter relates to the weakening of chemical bonds as a result of the photoexcitation [24]. However, there is a strong disagreement in the field regarding the existence and the exact mechanisms of the two effects. Some claims that only photochemical effects should be considered, others suggest that photothermal effects are the sole cause of the reduction [13]. For instance, regarding the heat (i.e., thermal energy) generated in the laser-mater interactions, from the photothermal point of view, it is the driven force of laser-induced reduction of GO, however, as well known the recombination of excited electrons and holes would also generate heat. Although there is no consensus yet regarding the exact mechanism, increasing study are proposing that both photothermal and photochemical processes take place during the photon-induced reduction of GO [24]. Lastly, many of the existing research employed pulsed laser in their research, as explained in Section 2.1, pulse laser can generate electric field that ionize the atoms and molecules, although low-intensity lasers were applied in laser reduction of GO which does not mean ionization could be excluded and it could potentially provide a new fundamental angle to perceive the reaction mechanisms. So far no study yet investigates the laser-matter interactions from this angle.

LA is the most popular and widely adopted technique for synthesis of different nanomaterials in the form of nanopowder, films with nanoscale features or colloidal dispersions. LA is a flexible process that could be achieved in vacuum or liquid solutions with abundant choices of target materials [3, 5–8, 25, 26]. The typical setups for the two types of LA in gaseous and liquid phases are schematically outlined in Fig. 2 .

Gaseous-phase LA refers to making nanoparticles or nanostructured films through evaporation of a solid target and subsequent deposition in vacuum using short pulsed (e.g., femtosecond laser) laser. Liquid-phase LA are increasingly becoming popular due to the simplicity of the processing, the variety of the nanomaterials can be synthesized, and the capability of making stable colloidal dispersions without the introduction of chemical ligands or foreign chemical agents (e.g., surfactants, wrapping polymers, etc.). These features make it an extremely competitive alternative to the conventional synthesis routes to nanomaterials via wet chemistry which usually needs the presence of chemical ligands or wrapping agents such as surfactants or polymers that are able to form micelles [27–31].

Regardless the types of LA, this process normally needs a solid target material upon which a pulsed laser beam is applied. The LA is a dynamic process taking place on the time scale of femtosecond to microsecond. Although the exact mechanisms for LA are very complicated and still under continuous investigation [25], it mainly involves the photon-induced material ionization, plasma phase formation, and the subsequent nucleation of nanoparticles upon the recombination of plasma usually in the form of vaporization (gaseous LA) or burst (liquid-phase LA). In another word, the laser radiation could cause the ejection of a very small volume of materials from the surface of the solid target material in the form of plasma plume which eventually condenses to form nanoscale particles.

As ionization and plasma formation are critical for LA, only short-pulsed lasers such as nanosecond-, picosecond-, and preferably femtosecond lasers are usually applied. In contrast, millisecond pulsed laser or continuous laser mainly induces thermal energy, therefore, they are not suitable for LA [3, 25]. Moreover, among nanosecond-, picosecond-, and femtosecond lasers, femtosecond lasers are the most ideal sources for LA as not just because it can further reduce the influence of photothermal effects also because the plasma generated with a femtosecond laser are fundamentally different from the rest [3].

So far, an extensive collection of nanomaterials has been created by LA stretching across different tribes such as metal nanoparticles, oxides nanoparticles, semiconductor nanoparticles, and carbon-based materials. These materials have been considered for a range of industrial applications across electronics, catalysis, energy conversion, biology, biomedical, automotive, and construction. This section focuses on the metal, metal oxide, and semiconductor nanoparticles made available by LA. Since Section 5 is dedicated to the laser-enabled synthesis and processing of carbon-based nanomaterials, they are not included in this part of discussion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal nanoparticles from LA and applications
ce:section.ce:para: So far, many metal nanoparticles are made available via LA especially in the liquid phase [6, 25, 32, 33]. The liquid-phase LA possess an excellent advantage over the conventional wet chemistry methods as it can directly produce nanoparticles that form stable colloidal dispersions without the need of any stabilizing agents (e.g., surfactants or chemical ligands) that are usually required by wet chemistry methods [33, 34]. Noble metals such as gold (Au), silver (Ag), platinum (Pt), ruthenium (Ru), and palladium (Pd) were among the first successfully synthesized by LA [35]. Nanoparticles of conventional metals such as copper (Cu), nickel (Ni), and zinc (Zin) can also be easily prepared by LA [33, 34]. Apart from pure metals, LA is also employed for the synthesis of alloy nanoparticles, such as gold/silver (Au/Ag), platinum/iron (Pt/Fe), gold/iron (Au/Fe), nickel/ruthenium (Ni/Ru), nickel/titanium (Ni/Ti), and so on [33].

Metal nanoparticles have many application perspectives. First of all, when light interacts with metal nanoparticles, an electromagnetic filed propagating along the surface of the metal particles is generated. This phenomenon is known as localized surface plasma resonance (LSPR) [36, 37]. LSPR is a valuable feature that has been applied for the enhancement of signal in Raman spectroscopy and photoluminecence [37–40]. Another important application for LSPR is optical sensing in analytical chemistry and biosensing either through direct utilization (i.e., response of plasmonic bands to analytes) or enhancing optical signals (e.g., fluorescence) [37, 41, 42]. Besides, photothermal therapy is a highly recommended application area for plasmonic metal particles [42, 43]. Metal nanoparticles from LA have been frequently applied in conjunction with other materials to form efficient functional materials such as catalysts [44, 45] and energy storage devices [46]. As metal nanoparticles have intrinsic electric conductivity, they have been directly used or as conductive fillers in polymers-based composites for flexible sensors (e.g., strain sensor) [47, 48]. Lastly with the rise of printable electronics and additive manufacturing (i.e., 3D printing), metal nanoparticles are also important ingredients for formulation of conductive inks for different 2D (e.g., screen printing, roller printing, etc.) and 3D printing (e.g., direct writing). [48–51]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal oxide nanoparticles from LA and applications
ce:section.ce:para: Metal oxides nanoparticles are another branch of nanoparticles that can be directly made by LA and they are frequently synthesized and intensively studied. A wide range of metal oxide nanoparticles have been synthesized using metal targets including iron (Fe) [52, 53], aluminum (Al) [54], titanium (Ti), zinc (Zn) [55], nickel (Ni), copper (Cu), tin (Sn), cobalt (Co), magnesium (Mg), cerium (Ce), bismuth (Bi), yttrium (Y), gadolinium (Gd), etc. [53, 56, 57].

The most prospective and also intensively studied application of oxide nanoparticles from LA is for different catalysis processes including catalysis in common chemical reactions, photocatalysis [58, 59], and electrocatalysis [60–62]. Apart from catalysis, some oxide nanoparticles possess special electronic and magnetic properties allowing them to be applicable in specific areas. For instance, titanium oxide nanoparticles possess a wide electronic bandgap (~3.2eV) and the position of its conduction band makes it an ideal electron conduction materials for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [63–65]. Zinc oxide nanoparticles possess characteristic piezoelectric properties which have been utilized for nanogenerators that can harvest energy from low-frequency motion (e.g., human motion) for wearable electronics [66]. Iron oxide nanoparticles have unique magnetic properties (e.g., “superparamagnetism”) making them very useful in biology and biomedical for in vivo and in vitro studies as well as diagnostic and therapeutic applications (e.g., drug delivery) [67–71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Semiconductor nanoparticles from LA and applications
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional semiconductors such as silicon (Si), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and so on are synthesized by using their bulk materials as target in LA [25, 72]. Another important branch of semiconductor nanoparticles also easily attainable via LA are the II–IV compounds including cadmium tellurium (CdTe), cadmium sulfide (CdS), cadmium selenium (CdSe), and zinc tellurium (ZnTe) [73, 74] which are often the subjects dominate by wet chemistry-based methods.

Regarding applications, silicon nanoparticles are good candidates for optoelectronics, photovoltaic devices (i.e., solar cells), and memory devices [25, 72, 75]. The II–IV compounds have even wider spectrum of potential use [76–80]. As a matter of fact, these compounds nanoparticles have been one of the major topics among the community of nanoscale materials and the device engineering. The reason for their popularity is the highly tunable physical properties thanks to the so-called “quantum confinement” effects which make it easy to adjust the bandgap, electronic, and optical properties (e.g., color of emission) easily through tuning the particle size [77, 81, 82]. As a result, these semiconductor nanoparticles also bear another name “quantum dots” which are especially suitable for optoelectronics such as light-emitting diode (LED) displays [83–85]. In 2017, new high definition LED displays based on semiconductor quantum dots have already been successfully commercialized and released to market by Samsung (i.e., QLED).

Laser pyrolysis is another strategy developed for the synthesis of nanoscale materials. Although it is not as popular as LA, it gained certain degree of attention especially for the synthesis of carbide materials (e.g., SiC, TiC, ZrC) [86] and ceramic nanoparticles (e.g., TiO2, SiO2) [87].

In a typical laser pyrolysis process (Fig. 3 ), the gaseous-phase precursors are introduced to a chamber by a carrier gas (e.g., argon) where the gaseous-phase precursors meet the laser beam. The high-power laser beam (e.g., 2400W) generates elevated localized temperatures which trigger the nucleation and growth of nanoparticles. The nanoparticles are then collected by a catcher equipped with a filter. The laser pyrolysis is characterized as a gaseous-phase process, which does not mean liquids reactants are not allowed. Where liquid reactants are necessary, those reactants can be vaporized or turned into microscale droplets (e.g., via an ultra-sonication process) thereby they can be introduced to the reaction chamber. Since this technique mainly applied the thermal energy induced by high-power lasers, lasers (e.g., CO2) operate in continuous modes are often applied.

Nowadays, optical projection lithography is the state-of-the-art processing technique for the semiconductor industry which makes most of the revolutionary electronics and information technologies available for us. The adoption of optical photolithography for semiconductor industry could be dated back to the late 1950s [88]. It started by applying normal ultraviolet (UV) from mercury lamp that quickly replaced by UV lasers thanks to the improved precision [88, 89]. For instance, the characteristic size (R) was reduced to under 100nm from above 500nm [12] by applying UV lasers. Fig. 4 schematically presented a typical set of laser-based projection optical lithography. To continuously improve the resolution and reduce the characteristic size so that faster devices (e.g., CPU) can be realized, applying shorter wavelength UV lasers becomes crucial. Currently, the deep UV (e.g., ArF laser with wavelength=193nm) lithography is well established for creating features below 100nm [12, 89]. Further reduction of node size has been achieved by extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography. In 2014, the first 14nm chips were released to the market by Intel with the EUV-based lithography. Now, the industry is striving to achieve 7nm node features to create finer and faster devices till the end of Moore’s Law [90, 91].

In contrast to the laser projection optical nanolithography, laser interference nanolithography does not require a premade photomask. Instead, it enables nanoscale patterning via interference of the laser beam [12, 92–94]. Specifically, the beam of a laser is separated into two (or more beams) [93] one of which is then reflected to interfere with the other via applying a mirror as illustrated in Fig. 5 . This technique has the advantage of creating large-scale periodical nanoscale structures [92, 95, 96], however, lacks the patterning freedom a photomask can offer.

In addition to the two major laser-enabled techniques for nanofabrication introduced above, other types of laser-assisted nanofabrication methods have attracted interests of research and development include laser nanoimprint lithography [97, 98] and laser-assisted growth [e.g., laser-assisted chemical vapor deposition (CVD)] [10]. These varieties are able to create nanoscale features and patterns, however, still under active development. The productivity and cost aspects restricting their immediate adoption by industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Laser-driven synthesis and processing of carbon-based nanomaterials and applications
ce:section.ce:para: If one has to name one element which is so important for our life and also is at the current forefront of research and technology development at the nanoscale, carbon must be a popular choice.

In addition to be one of the main fossil energy sources, carbon-based conventional materials have already been applied in a very broad range of applications including composites (e.g., carbon black in rubber for tyres), energy storage (e.g., graphite for battery electrodes), air and water purification (e.g., active carbon), etc. Carbon-based nanoscale materials including CNTs, graphene and derivatives (e.g., GO, GQDs, and graphene nanoribbon), NDs, and CDs are very dynamic fields for research and development which are on course to bring revolutionary technologies and products ranging from faster electronics, energy conversion and storage, biology, biomedical, and environmental remediation. Laser happens to be one of the major driving forces underline the synthesis and processing of these carbon-based nanomaterials. Therefore, we devote this section in particular to introducing the adoption of laser in synthesis, processing, and applications of these carbon-based nanomaterials.

CNTs are first reported in 1991 by Ijima et al. [99]. Abundant research efforts have been drawn to it ever since. There are two forms of CNTs, single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). In general, SWCNTs are superior to MWCNTs in many aspects [100].

CNTs can be made by LA amongst a number of methods including CVD [101], arc discharge [99, 102, 103], etc. Similar to all the peers, LA also requires the presence of catalysts to guide the growth of CNT [104–106]. It is not competitive with respect to the cost and quality of products because it requires highly purified graphite target and elevated laser power but only produces nonuniform CNTs [105, 106].

Besides synthesis, laser has also been continuously applied for the processing of CNTs-based materials especially through laser sintering and laser polymerization for creating different functional or structural composite materials. Through the process of laser sintering CNTs are incorporated into ceramic [107, 108], metallic [109–111], and polymer [112–114] matrices to created composite functional materials with improved properties such as thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, optical absorption, energy storage capacity, rheology, etc. CNTs/Ti nanocomposites having excellent mechanical strength and potentially applicable as structural materials in aerospace and automobile industries can also been created by laser sintering process [115]. Laser polymerization at the nanoscale is an active research field thanks to its great potential in nano- and microfabrication [15]. There have been demonstrations on applying laser polymerization for making different 3D microscale or nanoscale features of CNT/polymer composite materials [14, 116]. Besides, either laser sintering or laser polymerization technique is currently applied in the 3D printing industry, which makes CNTs-based composite materials very promising for the rising additive manufacturing.

Graphene was discovered in 2004 [117] which is the first 2D material ever found stable at ambient conditions. Actually, 2D material consisted of just one layer of carbon atoms was theoretically predicted unstable. The discovery of graphene rectified this misbelief and also triggered an active search for similar atomic thick materials thereby creating a new rich family of 2D materials including graphene and derivatives, 2D oxides, 2D dichalcogenides, 2D metal-organic frameworks, and Mxene [118–120]. Therefore, the two physicists who discovered graphene were awarded Nobel Prize in 2010.

Although laser-based techniques have been applied for the synthesis of graphene resemblances [121, 122], compared to other mainstream methods such as CVD [123], epitaxial growth [124], and wet chemistry routes [125], the laser-based methods are not so advantageous. Nevertheless, laser has become a very effective tool for processing and device engineering of graphene-based materials.

The first demonstration of the potential of laser for processing and device engineering for graphene-related materials was carried out by two separate research groups in early 2010 [23, 126]. Both group applied laser to convert GO into patterned reduced graphene. Ever since, it has become a dynamic research field with many important topics on materials development, device engineering, and fundamental understanding of laser-induced reactions during the processing (e.g., laser direct writing on GO or polymer surface).

Following the initial work, scientists have demonstrated the potential of this technology for cost-effective fabrication of graphene electrodes for highly functional flexible super capacitors even with a cheap (~$20USD) optical CD driver having the LightScribe function [127]. Many work has also been carried out to demonstrate the flexibility of this method [13, 20, 128]. Regarding the type of lasers, both continuous lasers and pulsed lasers were adopted for the reduction of GO and simultaneously patterning for devices. There is also study shows the laser-induced reduction and patterning are independent of substrates [129]. Although single focused beam is a popular choice, there is also report on using interference of two laser beams for the reduction of GO [130]. The properties of the laser-derived graphene materials (e.g., surface chemistry, electrical conductivity, charge mobility, etc.) from laser scribing of GO is highly related to the parameters of laser including power, scanning speed, and types of lasers [13, 20, 128]. Besides, it is also very convenient to use laser to make functionalized or chemically doped graphene materials simply by in situ laser irradiation of the mixture of reactants and GO precursor [131]. Similar strategy can also be applied to synthesize graphene-based composite materials [132]. Another point worth mentioning is although 2D patterning are the most frequent demonstration in literature, recent studies show that it is also viable to create 3D structures with the laser direct writing [133–135]. Thanks to the direct patterning feature, laser-derived graphene and composites are well suited for different electronics and energy devices [13, 20, 128].

In spite of the great potential for industrial applications, the only aspect lag behind the rapid development of the materials and devices is the fundamental understanding of the mechanisms behind the laser-induced reduction of GO. Hitherto, two main explanations proposed in literatures are: photothermal and photochemical effects. There is no consensus yet.

The discovery of NDs dates back to the 1960s [136, 137]. The recent surge of research popularity in this particular material is due to the experimental achievement of long-sought nitrogen vacancies [138] embedded in its crystalline structure which give the NDs their excellent PL [139, 140] and the separation of sub 10nm primary particles from its secondary agglomerations via wet chemistry [141, 142]. NDs are also quasispherical crystalline particles mainly consisted of sp3 carbon (i.e., diamond structure). Although NDs normally prone to form agglomerates as large as few hundred nanometers or even over micron meters in the raw states, the individual primary particles of NDs could be sub 10nm [142].

Although the main stream of method for synthesis of NDs is detonation [136, 137], LA can also make NDs from carbon targets such as graphite [143–146], amorphous carbon [147], etc. Both liquid phase [144–146, 148] and gaseous phase [143] LAs can produce nanocrystalline diamonds. The properties including crystal structure, phase, chemical and physical properties, and particle size are subject to the adjustment of synthesis conditions including the laser wavelength, power and pulse, and liquid solvents (e.g., water, organics solvents). In addition to synthesis, laser processing of NDs-based materials also caught certain attention, there have been demonstration such as modification of chemical and physical properties by laser irradiation [149] and micro-patterning by direct laser writing on nanocrystalline diamond films [150].

Like majority of carbon-based nanomaterials, NDs have a wide span of applications. So far, NDs have been demonstrated for bioimaging, nanomedicine, sensing, photovoltaics, photocatalysts, and LED [34, 151]. There is a great interest on NDs-based quantum information processing or quantum computing which might foster revolutionary devices [152]. For sensing in particular, a recent study shows that NDs can be used for optical detection of very trivial intracellular temperature gradient enabled by the spin-associated electron transitions [153].

CDs are accidentally isolated in the process of purification of SWCNTs [154]. CDs are a type of fluorescent nanocarbon normally are amorphous in nature and often considered having spherical shapes. Although a variety of methods have been developed for the synthesis of CDs, laser-based methods especially LA is among the most popular due to the flexibility of this method in tuning the features of the resulting CDs and the green aspect (e.g., no stabilizing agent is necessary which is desirable for biomedical applications).

CDs have been successfully synthesized frequently by LA in liquid media [155–161] and less frequently in vacuum (i.e., gaseous phase) [162]. The choices of precursors are flexible. Conventional target like graphite [155, 163, 164] is a popular choice. Other targets such as diamond films [165], active carbon [158], etc. have also been used. By varying the laser parameters, the size of CDs could be easily tuned [157]. Apart from pure CDs, chemically doped CDs such as nitrogen-doped CDs could also be obtained one step by LA [164]. In addition to ablation, CDs could also been synthesized by a laser fragmentation process in which the laser irradiation breaks large carbonaceous particles suspended in solutions into small luminescent CDs [166]. There is also a recent drive for incorporating such reaction in a continuous flow manner for scalable production of CDs [166, 167].

Thanks to the high biocompatibility and low toxicity of the photoluminescent CDs, the main interest surrounding their applications lies in the biomedical area for topics such as bio-imaging, bio-sensing as well as therapeutics (i.e., drug delivery) [34, 151, 168, 169]. Apart from that, similar to NDs, they have been proposed to optoelectronics [168, 170], photovoltaics [171], and photocatalysis [172].

GQDs are fragments of graphene (both single-layer and few-layer graphene) in the shape of 2D discs or flakes consisting of mainly sp2 carbon. Different from CDs, GQDs are crystalline particles. Their sizes are normally within 100nm. As a theoretical concept, GQDs are studied as early as 1996 [173]. The experimental studies of GQDs were made possible by the discovery of graphene [174]. In the early experimental works of GQDs, devices are made from electron beam lithography and quantum confinement was experimentally observed [174, 175]. The first wet chemistry synthesis of fluorescent GQDs came in 2010 when Pan et al. synthesized GQDs by cutting chemically reduced graphene with a hydrothermal method [176].

So far a range of methods have been developed for the synthesis of GQDs. These methods could be classified into two groups: top down and bottom up [177, 178]. The top-down methods apply bulk graphitic materials (e.g., graphene, graphite, carbon fiber, coal, CNT, etc.) as precursors then exfoliate and cut the size down to obtain GQDs by some common methods such as hydrothermal, heated in a mixture of acids or reflux in nitric acid [179–181]. Bottom-up methods start from molecular monomer precursors (e.g., citric acid, glucose, etc.) to assemble GQDs [182, 183]. Recently, LA is also increasingly proposed for the synthesis of GQDs. GQDs have been successfully made by LA with graphite [121, 184, 185], carbon nano-onion [186], CNT [187, 188], and GO dispersions [189] as targets. Nitrogen-doped GQDs from LA have been reported too [190].

Similar to NDs and CDs, GQDs have been proposed and demonstrated for bioimaging, sensing, photovoltaics, photocatalysts, and LED display [65, 178, 182, 191]. With respect to the biocompatibility, our recent review indicates that GQDs have lower toxicity than CDs, GO, and CNT [192] and their uptake by cell may be also more efficient. Besides those highly recommended applications. Defects free pristine GQDs are promising candidates for spintronics [193] one of the next-generation fast electronics under development.

One continuous critical problem in the field of GQDs is the lack of a unified understanding of the origin of their photoluminescence [194]. Most popular explanations are quantum confinement and defects states. Recently, we have proposed that the disorder structures (i.e., defects, functional groups, sp3 carbons) and the carbon cores all have their contribution to the photoluminescence of the GQDs [38, 180]. The understanding of the fundamental mechanisms behind the photoluminescence of GQDs is essential for property tuning and functional design for their expected industrial applications.

Laser is one of the greatest inventions of the 20th century. It has enabled a vast span of technologies and devices that have changed and are still continuously changing the world. In particular for nanomaterials and nanotechnologies, laser has been successfully applied in the synthesis and processing. Regarding synthesis, laser-based techniques such as LA, laser pyrolysis have been developed to produce a large family of nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, semiconductor nanoparticles as well as different carbon-based nanoparticles. These nanomaterials are well suited for a variety of important industrial applications ranging from structural materials, electronics, energy devices, sensors, catalysis to biology, and biomedical. With respect to the processing, laser-based nanofabrication has provided mankind many revolutionary technologies such as the laser projection lithography which is the dominant technology for the semiconductor industry to realize fast information process and computation devices such as smart phone and computers, which are still driving the industry toward finer features (e.g., 7nm chips). Lasers are also increasing applied for processing of new materials such as reduction and simultaneously patterning of GO to obtain different functional devices.

Looking beyond the achievements, we believe continuous work needs to address remaining challenges to fully unleash the potential of laser in the synthesis and processing of nanomaterials for different industrial applications. Regarding industrial applications, here we suggest four aspects that require further research and development. First, fundamental understanding of laser-matter interactions requires continuous research. For example, the fundamental processes underneath LA are not yet fully unveiled. The debate between the photothermal and photochemical effects behind the laser-induced reduction of GO is still continuing. Second, efforts should be placed on optimization and standardization of synthesis conditions for controllable and consistent synthesis of uniform nanomaterials with productivity and scalability matching the demand of industrial applications. Third, laser-based processing techniques (e.g., lithography) mainly focus on 2D patterning, 3D nanofabrication still needs significant improvement. Last but not the least, the integration of laser driven synthesis and processing of nanomaterials into existing industry processes or the independent commercialization of these techniques remain challenging.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials: Processing and Characterization With Lasers
J. Phys. B At. Mol. Opt. Phys.
Applications of Laser Ablation—Thin Film Deposition, Nanomaterial Synthesis and Surface Modification
Appl. Phys. A Mater. Sci. Process.
